topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud. My fingers, hovering over the keyboard, froze mid-stroke. I reread the subject line three times, a slow, deliberate motion that felt clumsy and heavy. The words swam before my eyes—"Scholarship Application Update." My heart began a frantic, uneven rhythm against my ribs. I clicked on it, and the stark, formal rejection letter filled the screen. A strange, hollow sensation washed over me, like the sudden drop in temperature on a summer day. I leaned back in my chair, the worn fabric groaning beneath me, and stared blankly at the ceiling tiles. What had I done wrong? Every meticulously crafted essay, every late-night revision session, suddenly seemed to dissolve into meaningless ink. A slow, shaky breath escaped my lungs.

[story 2]

I was sorting through old documents, a mundane task meant to be grounding, when the notification popped up. My hand, holding the stack of papers, actually trembled, causing a few sheets to shift precariously. I blinked hard, trying to process the stark, unforgiving text displayed on my monitor. My jaw tightened involuntarily. A sudden, cold emptiness spread through my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal against the floor echoing loudly in the quiet dorm room. I walked over to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the sprawling campus below, feeling utterly disconnected from the vibrant life happening just outside my window. The reality of the situation settled in, heavy and undeniable.

[story 3]

When I finally opened the file, I didn't immediately read the content. I just stared at the confirmation page, my gaze darting back and forth across the screen as if expecting the information to change. A sharp intake of breath hitched in my throat. My hands clenched into tight fists on the desk, the knuckles turning white. The silence in the room became oppressive, amplifying the frantic drumming inside my ears. Slowly, I forced my eyes to scan the text, absorbing the devastating news. A wave of disorientation washed over me; the world seemed momentarily tilted on its axis. I let out a small, involuntary sound—a weak exhale—and then simply sat there, utterly still, unable to move or process the sudden finality of the message.
